text: The surgeon inserts the optical trocar using the Scandinavian technique through an incision in the infraumbilical crease. The tract, identified as the obliterated vitellointestinal duct, is dilated using artery forceps, visibly opening the loose areolar tissue.

The surgeon uses the modified Scandinavian technique to directly insert the optical trocar, demonstrating the method of holding the ports. After insertion, the primary trocar is placed as the only working port in the suprapubic region under direct vision.

The surgeon positions their index finger parallel to the long axis of the port, pointing towards the skin to avoid injury. A grasper is inserted through the port, which will be the sole instrument used for manipulation and movements during the procedure.

The surgeon locates the appendix by following the tinea coli from the cecum. The appendix appears elongated and acutely inflamed. The surgeon prepares to insert the varus needle for dissection and appendectomy using a single working port.
A 2 mm incision is made for the insertion of the varus needle, with the insertion point varying based on the patient's anatomy. The needle is inserted gently to avoid injury from its sharp bevel.
The surgeon removes the stylet from the varus needle and passes a loop through the needle using a custom loop passer. A thread, specifically purlin, is used for this purpose.

The surgeon removes the loop passer and inserts the loop for easier handling. Using a grasper, the appendix is grasped and fed through the loop. The surgeon maintains visual focus on the tip of the varus needle to avoid puncture injuries to the bowel or other intraperitoneal structures. The surgeon then identifies the point for dissection.

The surgeon adjusts the holding position of the appendix by loosening the loop and re-grasping it at a more proximal point for easier manipulation. The appendix is rotated clockwise and held with the needle. Dissection is performed using Ligasure, and the surgeon plans to ligate the base of the appendix with an extracorporeal knot due to its length, which makes handling challenging.

The surgeon holds the appendix more proximally and continues the dissection towards its base. The appendix is attached to the lateral abdominal wall, requiring careful dissection. The surgeon uses the varus needle to cook and cut the remaining mesoappendix, freeing the appendix completely.

The surgeon brings in the thread end of Vicryl 1-0 for extracorporeal knotting, winding it around the base of the appendix. The knot is tightened using a knot pusher, securing the base of the appendix. The thread is then cut, completing the ligation step in the appendectomy assisted by the varus needle.

The surgeon cuts the appendix at a safe distance from the ligation point. Due to inflammation, the appendix cannot be removed through a 5mm port. Instead, the surgeon uses a grasper to feed the appendix into a 10mm optical trocar for removal.

The surgeon uses artery forceps to grasp the appendix, which is then easily pulled out. The optical trocar wound is washed with normal saline and pyridine to prevent infection. The post-operative view shows no visible optical trocar wound, and the working trocar wound is below the waistline.
